coredata.dc:title: Decoration of carbon nanodots on conjugated triazine framework nanosheets as Z-scheme heterojunction for boosting opto-electro photocatalytic degradation of organic hydrocarbons from petrochemical wastewater

coredata.dc:description:
Environmental concerns have arisen due to the persistent nature and carcinogenicity of organic hydrocarbons. In the following study, authors report the synthesis of a conjugated covalent triazine-based framework (CCTF-P) utilizing a perylene core anhydride derived from 3,4,9,10-perylenetetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA) as a linking agent with triazine-based structure that resulting Z-scheme CQD/CCTF-P hybrid photocatalyst was obtained by decorating CQDs (N-CQD, S-CQD, and N-S-CQD) onto the CCTF-P surfaces. CCTF-P nanosheets, N-CQD/CCTF-P, S-CQD/CCTF-P, and N-SCQD/CCTF-P, are used as photocatalysts to break down OHC when exposed to visible light. The obtained CCTF-P nanosheets demonstrated the narrowed band-gap and high-performance degradation of organic hydrocarbon (OHC), and the prepared N-SCQD/CCTF-P heterojunction displayed the narrowest band-gap and the highest photocatalytic performance with a rate constant of 1.69 h−1. The proposed photocatalytic activation mechanism was based on scavenger tests, indicating that
O
2
• −
, 1O2, e-,
and
OH
•
play a major role. The Z-scheme N-SCQD/CCTF-P heterojunction shows promise as a way to create new photocatalysts for using solar power to remove emerging contaminants due to its narrow bandgap, high crystalline structure, and photocatalytic performance.

FESEM images of CCTF-P (a), N-CQD/CCTF-P (b), S-CQD/CCTF-P (c), and N-SCQD/CCTF-P (d); TEM images of CCTF-P (e and g), N-SCQD/CCTF-P (f and h), and N-SCQD (i); EDX spectrums of CCTF-P (j), N-CQD/CCTF-P (k), S-CQD/CCTF-P (l), and N-SCQD/ CCTF-P (m); AFM image (n) and height profile (o) of CCTF-P nanosheets; and HRTEM images of N-SCQD/ CCTF-P (p and q).

Nyquist plot of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (a), transient photocurrent response (b), Motty-Schottky plot (c), estimation of the valence band edge by XPS (d), and proposal of bands alignment of CCTF-P and N-SCQD heterojunction (e).

Time-graph investigation of the breakdown of normalized concentration of OHC with CCTF-P, N-CQD/CCTF-P, S-CQD/CCTF-P, and N-SCQD/CCTF-P (a), the coefficient rates of the pseudo-first-order model for OHC reduction, kOHC (b) (V= 100 mL, pH = 7.0, T = 25 °C, CCQD/CCTF-P = 0.03 g L−1), the impact of various techniques (c), the impact of initial pH (d), the impact of catalyst mass (e), and the impact of temperature (f) for OHC degradation.
Scavenger effect on the temporal normalized concentration of OHC with N-SCQD/CCTF-P (a) and Schematic representation of reaction mechanism of N-SCQD/CCTF-P photocatalyst (b).

GC-MS analysis results of OHC in petrochemical wastewater at different times of the photocatalytic degradation process 0 minutes (a); 30 minutes (b); 60 minutes (c); and 90 minutes (d); (V= 100 mL, pH = 7.0, T = 25 °C, CCQD/CCTF-P = 0.03 g L−1).

Reusability experiments of photodegrading OHC with N-SCQD/CCTF-P (a), the evolution of the pseudo-first order rate constant with the reuse cycles (b), XRD pattern of N-SCQD/CCTF-P after the 5th reuse cycle (c), and XPS pattern of N-SCQD/CCTF-P after the 5th reuse cycle (d).
Presented pathways for the photocatalytic conversion of some OHC utilizing N-SCQD/CCTF-P.
Quality of petrochemical wastewater used in this study.
The acute and chronic toxic effects of OHC compound and some its derivatives on aquatic organisms, such as green algae, fish, and Daphnia, are measured in mg L−1*.
Comparison of the best photocatalyst systems for OHC degradation.

head.ce:title: Decoration of carbon nanodots on conjugated triazine framework nanosheets as Z-scheme heterojunction for boosting opto-electro photocatalytic degradation of organic hydrocarbons from petrochemical wastewater

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Environmental concerns have arisen due to the persistent nature and carcinogenicity of organic hydrocarbons. In the following study, authors report the synthesis of a conjugated covalent triazine-based framework (CCTF-P) utilizing a perylene core anhydride derived from 3,4,9,10-perylenetetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA) as a linking agent with triazine-based structure that resulting Z-scheme CQD/CCTF-P hybrid photocatalyst was obtained by decorating CQDs (N-CQD, S-CQD, and N-S-CQD) onto the CCTF-P surfaces. CCTF-P nanosheets, N-CQD/CCTF-P, S-CQD/CCTF-P, and N-SCQD/CCTF-P, are used as photocatalysts to break down OHC when exposed to visible light. The obtained CCTF-P nanosheets demonstrated the narrowed band-gap and high-performance degradation of organic hydrocarbon (OHC), and the prepared N-SCQD/CCTF-P heterojunction displayed the narrowest band-gap and the highest photocatalytic performance with a rate constant of 1.69 h−1. The proposed photocatalytic activation mechanism was based on scavenger tests, indicating that
O
2
• −
, 1O2, e-,
and
OH
•
play a major role. The Z-scheme N-SCQD/CCTF-P heterojunction shows promise as a way to create new photocatalysts for using solar power to remove emerging contaminants due to its narrow bandgap, high crystalline structure, and photocatalytic performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the last few decades, an excessive explosion of the oil and petrochemical industries has resulted in enhanced quantities of water polluted with intricate compositions, such as aromatic hydrocarbons and aliphatic hydrocarbons, toxic phenolic rings, common inorganic dissolved solids, naphthenic derivatives, sulfide compounds, nitrogenous organic compounds, heavy metals, and solids [1–4]. Organic compounds, especially polycyclic aromatic organic compounds, are commonly persistent and have been classified as priority contaminants because of their genotoxic [5–7], mutagenic, carcinogenic, and estrogenic effects on humans and other organisms in all types of aquatic habitats [8,9].

In this way, it is important to discover how to beneficially remove organic hydrocarbon (OHC) from water [4,10–14]. To date, the photocatalytic degradation of OHC has gained a lot of attention as an effective and eco-friendly treatment method owing to its high efficiency [15,16], mild operation conditions [17–19], and lack of secondary pollution [20–23]. Accordingly, organic semiconductor-based photocatalysts such as linear conjugated polymers [24], covalent triazine-based frameworks (CTFs) [25], conjugated porous polymers (CMPs) [26,27], and also covalent porous organic frameworks (COFs) [28–31] have been studied for the degradation of pollutants as free metal photocatalysts with supernatural efficiency in photocatalytic processes under visible-light irradiation, owing to their high porosities [32], unique π-conjugated electronic structures [33], excellent chemical and thermal stabilities [34], and potential application prospects [33,35]. Among the covalently linked conjugated structures, highly crystalline [36] and triazine linkages [37] make CTFs effective as metal-free catalysts, while also being environmental-friendly due to the absence of leaching the metal phase [26,38]. In addition, the optical and electronic structures of polymers can be tailored by combining them with other semiconductors, such as carbon dots (CDs), which are novel carbon-based nanomaterials with sizes below 10 nm [39,40]. CDs have attracted widespread attention owing to their ideal optical and electronic features [41]. In contrast, CDs reveal excellent electron transfer [42], electron reservoirs, and photoluminescence attributes [43]. When CDs are coupled with polymers, the photocatalysts can generate more photogenerated charge carriers [44], thereby improving the photocatalytic efficiency by the diminution of electron-hole recombination processes and the expansion of the area of photo-adsorption [45].

In this study, we designed and prepared a conjugated polymer with covalent triazine-based framework (CCTF-P) nanosheets for the first time by linking the core units of 3,4,9,10-Perylenetetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA) with triazine (HTD)-based amines and bonding the molecular units together with strong covalent bonds. The configuration of the extremely delocalized π-conjugated arrangement, characterized by robust intermolecular π–π interactions, not only effectively facilitated the photo-electrical response and light harvesting in the visible light region by developing the wavelength [46], but also the process formed a quick way to produce highly reactive superoxide radicals (O2 •‾) and singlet oxygen (O2) and established an effective pathway through intermolecular charge transport and energy transfer [47,48]. The synthesized polymer exhibited remarkable photocatalytic performance for the photodegradation of OHC exposed to visible light. Nevertheless, the degradation of OHC using CCTF-P is still not as good, likely because they are not very efficient at capturing solar energy and have quick charge recombination rate. To overcome the stated limitation, we decorated the CCTF-P nanosheet's surface with CQDs (N-CQD, S-CQD, and N-S-CQD) to propose a novel Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst for the breaking down of OHC exposed to visible-light. Because, N atoms have a different electronegativity from carbon (N = 3.04 compare with C= 2.55), but S atoms have a closer electronegativity (2.58) than carbon atoms, which may significantly help electron migration from the nearby C atoms, resulting in a high level of charge density of the C atoms. Incorporating S or N heteroatoms containing into the carbon structure can enhance the material's electrical conductivity, catalytic efficiency, and stability of chemicals. Moreover, if both N and S heteroatoms are doped together into carbon, there might be a synergistic effect, as noted by previous researchers. Actually, in certain situations, adding both S and N to carbon materials can boost the number of catalytic sites via improving the redistribution of charge and spin within the carbon structure [49,50]. The appearance morphology and chemical framework of the CCTF-P, N-CQD/CCTF-P, S-CQD/CCTF-P, and N-SCQD/CCTF-P were investigated via transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), fourier transform infrared (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), Brunauer–Emmett Teller, Zeta potential, ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), Photoluminescence (PL), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), Photocurrent, Mott-Schottky, and band structures analyses. The effects of sono-degradation, pH, photocatalyst dose, and temperature on the OHC degradation were systematically studied. Ultimately, the conceivable OHC degradation ways were perused via gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) analysis, and the mechanism of degradation of this photocatalytic process was examined via quenching experiments and electrochemical assays.

The utilized chemical compounds were derived from Merck and did not undergo any further refinement. Double distilled water with resistivity approximately 18.2 µS cm−1 from the Millipore device was exerted for the entire duration of the experiments. Petrochemical wastewater was collected from the Pars Special Economic Energy Zone in the Province of Bushehr, Iran, and immediately transferred to the laboratory and stored at 4 ◦C after measuring the physio-chemical aspects. The quality of wastewater is presented in Table 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Production of conjugated covalent triazine framework polymer nanosheets (CCTF-P)
ce:section.ce:para: CCTF-P was synthesized in two steps. In the first stage, a combination of 20 mL hydrazine hydrate (80 %) and 20 mL acetonitrile was slowly added to a mixture of 20 mmol of cyanuric chloride in 50 mL of acetonitrile and sonicated at 50 °C for about 1 hour. Afterward, the solvent and residue hydrazine were eliminated, and 40 mL of diethyl ether was added. The obtained white powder was collected, washed with diethyl ether (100 mL) three times, and in the following vacuum dried at 60 ◦C approximately 24 hours. Eventually, 2.0 g of pure product (HTD) was obtained with a yield of 96 %-98 % [51].

The CCTF-P provided by adding 1.0 mmol of HTD (0.171 g), 1.5 mmol (0.588 g) of PTCDA, and 1.5 mmol zinc acetate (0.275 g) to 5.0 g of imidazole as a solvent in a 100 mL flask and stirred and heated at 180 °C under N2 for 12 h. After cooling at room temperature, the combination dispersed in HCl (100 mL, 1 mol L−1), and mixed about 8 h. The red substance was collected via vacuum filtering using a porous membrane with a pore size of 0.45 µm and subsequently purified with double distilled water to remove any further chloride ions from the rinsing water. Ultimately, the washed solid was treated via dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) so that it became almost free of color, a process that was repeated three times. The collected product was then freeze-dried for a period of 24 h. In the end, 0.634 g of CCTF-P was obtained with a yield of 89.2 % ( Fig. 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of carbon quantum nanodots (NCQD, S-CQD, and N-S-CQD)
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal process.

For N-CQD: 2 mmol (0.384 g) of citric acid were introduced to 15 mL double distilled water in a crystal beaker under continuous stirring. Afterward, 6 mmol (0.365 g) of melamine was gradually added to the above solution and then mixed for 30 min, resulting in the formation of a homogeneous solution and the mixture was then heated at 200 °C for a duration of 5 h. Afterward, the system was subsequently allowed to reach ambient condition, centrifugated at 14000 rpm about 15 min, and then subjected to diffusion in a dialysis apparatus for 2 days. Finally, some N-CQD solution obtained was preserved at 4 °C in order to further application, and final solution was dried around 48 h in the freeze dryer [52,53].

For S-CQD: 2 mmol (0.384 g) of citric acid were introduced to 15 mL double distilled water in a crystal beaker under continuous stirring. Afterward, 6 mmol (0.486 g) of sodium sulfide was gradually added to the above solution and then mixed for 30 min, resulting in the formation of a homogeneous solution and the mixture was then heated at 200 °C for a duration of 5 h [54]. Therefore, in the same way as for the N-CQD.

For N-S-CQD: 2 mmol (0.384 g) of citric acid were introduced to 15 mL double distilled water in a crystal beaker under continuous stirring. Afterward, 6 mmol (0.456 g) of thiourea was gradually added to the above solution and then mixed for 30 min, resulting in the formation of a homogeneous solution and the mixture was then heated at 200 °C for a duration of 5 h [55]. From this point on, similarly to the N-CQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Production of conjugated covalent triazine framework polymer nanosheets decorated with doped carbon dots (CQDs/CCTF-P)
ce:section.ce:para: Specifically, 200 mg of CCTF-P spread out into 20 mL ethanol using ultrasonic device for a period of 30 min. Then, the mixture was stirred for approximately 1 h after adding 1.5 mL of CQDs solution. The combined solution was then introduced inside a Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at a temperature of 120 °C for approximately 2 h. When the temperature of the sample had reached ambient condition, the centrifuge was employed to separate the precipitate from the suspension. Subsequently, the synthesized material was purified on three times through the use of double distilled water before being freeze-dried for 24 h to produce CQDs/CCTF-P (Fig. 1). The prepared photocatalysts were designated as N-CQDs/CCTF-P, S-CQDs/CCTF-P, and N-SCQDs/CCTF-P, in accordance with the variety of carbon nanodots.

Some structural, surface morphological, electrical and optical characteristics of the CCTF-P, N-CQD/CCTF-P, S-CQD/CCTF-P, and N-SCQD/CCTF-P were considered using various techniques. The FT-IR and XRD patterns of various photocatalyst preparation processes were recorded using a Bruker Spectrum Tensor II (Germany) and an X-ray diffractometer Philips PW 3710 (UK), respectively. AFM of CCTF-P was carried out using an AFM Nanowizard (Germany). A BELSORP-mini II (USA) apparatus is employed to estimate the surface area and quantify the porosity of photocatalyst samples through the measurement of N2 adsorption-desorption at a temperature of −196 °C. XPS of the N-SCQD/CCTF-P was carried out on an electron spectrometer from Kratos AXIS UltraDLD (UK). TGA was executed with a heating rate of 20 ◦C min−1 in an N2 atmosphere on a PerkinElmer Diamond (USA) TG/DTA thermal analyzing device. Two microscopes were employed in these experiments: a Philips CM-120, which was used to conduct TEM, and also a ZEISS Sigma 300, which was utilized in SEM. The specific optical attributes were analyzed on a Hitachi U-3300 spectrophotometer (Japan) with the use of ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS). PL analysis was completed in a Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer of Agilent (USA) at an excitation wavelength (EX) of 300 nm. The electrochemical features of CCTF-P, N-CQD/CCTF-P, S-CQD/CCTF-P, and N-SCQD/CCTF-P including photocurrent, Mott-Schottky technique, and EIS were accomplished in a conventional three-electrode cell with 0.2 M Na2SO4 (pH = 7.0) as a solution containing electrolytes. A clear conductive glass sheet electrode is designed for operation in which indium-tin-oxide (ITO) has been introduced. Subsequently, a Pt plate wire and the mercury/mercurous chloride solution known as auxiliary classic and reference electrodes, respectively. An autolab PGSTAT-20 instrument (USA) was executed for electrochemical analyses. The OHC was quantified through the utilization of a GC-MS apparatus (Agilent, USA), furnished with a capillary column (DB-5 MS UI), and also operated with a carrier gas (He) with a flow speed of 1 mL min−1. The oven was initially adjusted to 100 °C and allowed to stabilize for approximately 1 min. It was then gradually enhanced to 210 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min, subsequently elevated to 280 °C at the same rate and kept at 280 °C for 20 min. During the OHC photocatalytic operation, chemical oxygen demand (COD) was analyzed according to standard methods [56].

The photodegradation characteristics of the prepared CCTF-P, N-CQD/CCTF-P, S-CQD/CCTF-P, and N-SCQD/CCTF-P were evaluated for OHC degradation in petrochemical wastewater utilizing a xenon light source (300 W) equipped with 380˗780 nm cut-off filters. In a typical photocatalytic reaction, 0.03 g L−1 of prepared photocatalysts were dispersed in 100 mL of the petrochemical wastewater in a Pyrex photoreactor (100 mL) and next the suspension magnetically agitated for 30 min in dark environment to establish adsorption equilibrium. After this step, the lamp was switched on the sample, the suspension was purged with air during the process at temperature of 25 ◦C using flowing cooling water system. In order to obtain representative samples, 3.5 mL of suspension was carefully collected at different time points (0–4 h) and purified using cellulose-based filters (Whatman, 0.22μm) for subsequent COD and gas chromatograph (GC-Mass) analysis. Moreover, the pH of the basic solution was altered by the introduction of HCl and/or NaOH (0.1 mol L−1) solutions. The objective of the quenching tests was to ascertain the efficacy of 1.0 mM benzoquinone (BQ) at quenching
O
2
• −
, 1.0 mM sodium azide (NaN3) function as a 1O2 inhibitor, and 10 mM tert-butanol (TBA) was employed in inactive
OH
•
, while silver nitrate (AgNO3, 1 mM) as an e- quencher, and 10 mM ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium (EDTA-2Na) was used to facilitate the inhibition of h+.

The program entitled 'ecological structure-activity relationship' (ECOSAR) was employed in order to ascertain the degree of acute and chronic toxicity exhibited by the OHC. The software enables the theoretical estimation of acute toxicities about the fatal or effective dose (LC or EC). Additionally, it permits the representation of long-term toxicities through the use of chronic values (ChV).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties of the CCTF-P and CQDs/CCTF-P heterojunction
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of CCTF-P, N-CQD/CCTF-P, S-CQD/CCTF-P, and N-SCQD/CCTF-P were investigated by FESEM, TEM, AFM, and EDX observations, as shown in Fig. 2a-l. The FESEM image shows that CCTF-P has a nanosheet shape and consisting of small thin and flat nanosheets. As shown in Fig. 2b–d, there are significant differences in the SEM morphology of the CQDs/CCTF-P prepared above, indicating that the type of CQDs and their distribution on the CCTF-P may affect the morphology, while all of them are in nanosheet form. Compared with the CCTF-P, the N-CQD/CCTF-P and N-SCQD/CCTF-P samples show a 3D staggered network architecture with increased porosity is advantageous for light capture as it facilitates multiple photonic reflections and the slow-photon effect (Fig. 2b and d). S-CQD/CCTF-P is assembled in irregular island-like nanosheets (Fig. 2c). Fig. 2e and g illustrate that the CCTF-P has a nanosheet structure, with clear polymer nanolayers and sharp edges. The 2D structure consists of small, narrow and flat nanosheets with folds, confirming the thin sheet property observed in TEM images.

Fig. 2f and h show that, for a specimen of CQDs/CCTF-P, CQDs nanoparticles (red circles) are successfully attached on the outer surface of CCTF-P with spherical shape, highly monodispersed, and a mean diameter distribution of close to 4.5 nm (Fig. 2i and S1), which may arise from π–π stacking interactions. EDX (Fig. 2j-m) and FEEM-EDX elemental mapping (Fig. S2) both support evidence for the existence of elements C, N, O, and S. Furthermore, the elemental mapping also demonstrates that CQDs NPs are homogeneously distributed on CCTF-P. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) analyses also exhibited the irregular sheet-like morphology of CCTF-P showing that the majority of these nanosheets are single-layers or double-layers with an average thickness of about 10 and 45 nm because of the strong π-π stacking between layers. For larger flakes, the height could be as high as ∼82 nm (Fig. 2n and o). HRTEM image demonstrated that CCTF-P was crystalline in nature with clear lattice fringe (Fig. 2q) and the distance between the adjacent crystal lattice is about 0.48 nm. Additionally, HRTEM images of Fig. 2p and q confirm the formation of spherical CQDs on the CCTF-P structure and well-defined quantum dot lattice morphologies inside the nanosheets. Also, Fig. 2q demonstrates the lattice spacing of 0.25 nm which corresponds to the (100) facet of graphitic carbon.

TGA of the CCTF-P was accomplished utilizing a stable flow of N2 gas with a rate of 10 °C min–1 at 25 −800 °C by heating the CCTF-P, and the result indicates that the CCTF-P is thermally stable at decompose temperatures up to 495 °C ( Fig. 3a). Of course, when heated from 375 °C to 495 °C, a weak weight loss of 24 % has occurred. The thermal stability of CCTF-P at a temperature of 800 °C is about 62.9 %, which indicates the remarkable thermal stability of CCTF-P. The findings were paralleled by other researchers. In 2023, the research group led by Jin demonstrated that COFs exhibited thermal stability up to 300 °C [57]. Also, Xu and et al. (2022) demonstrated that weight loss in triazine-based porous organic polymer can be seen at the temperature of 500 °C [58]. Moreover, compared with CCTF-P, the N-CQD/CCTF-P, S-CQD/CCTF-P, and N-SCQD/CCTF-P show a more weight loss due to decomposition of carbon dots.

Fig. 3b pretends the adsorption/desorption curves of CCTF-P, N-CQD/CCTF-P, S-CQD/CCTF-P, and N-SCQD/CCTF-P, and according to the IUPAC classification, the isotherm describes a type III isotherm and displays a weak adsorbent-adsorbate interaction in a range of P/P
0 = 0.02–0.99, suggesting that CCTF-P, N-CQD/CCTF-P, S-CQD/CCTF-P, and N-SCQD/CCTF-P are a macroporous solids. As demonstrated in Table S1, the measured surface areas of CCTF-P, N-CQD/CCTF-P, S-CQD/CCTF-P, and N-SCQD/CCTF-P were about 52.20, 7.79, 6.63, and 6.37 m2 g−1, and the total pore volumes are 0.388, 0.05, 0.017, and 0.011 cm3 g−1 at P/P
0 = 0.99, respectively. In addition, most of the macroporous were located within 0.4–2.0 nm, with a highest found at approximately 0.8 nm for CCTF-P. Subsequently, CQDs nanoparticles loaded on the CCTF-P surface, which led to decreasing the surface area and porosity of N-CQD/CCTF-P, S-CQD/CCTF-P, and N-SCQD/CCTF-P. Therefore, the impact of surface area of CCTF-P on photocatalytic performance could be mostly disregarded.

The crystalline structure of CCTF-P, N-CQD/CCTF-P, S-CQD/CCTF-P, and N-SCQD/CCTF-P was considered by powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD). As exhibited in Fig. 3c, the XRD data for CCTF-P indicates that the minor peaks are at 2θ = 8.0° (d=10.9 A°), 2θ = 11.5° (d = 7.6 A°), and 2θ = 14.9° (d = 5.9 A°), indicative of (110) and (200) facets of CCTF-P, implied that the polymer backbone was extended two-dimensionally [47].

The level of molecular crystallinity and the arrangement of π-π stacking structure is determined by measuring the d-spacing at 3.4 A°, which suggests the presence of a more extensive π-electron conjugated system that this occurrence may be attributed to the juxtaposition of delocalized electron clouds in the context of a face-to-face overlap [36]. It is seen that no clear different pattern was illustrated between CCTF-P, N-CQDs/CCTF-P, S-CQDs/CCTF-P, and N-SCQDs/CCTF-P photocatalysts. The only noteworthy observation is the reduction of the intensity in diffracted peaks in relation to CCTF-P, which may be attributed to the attachment of CQDs.

The surface charge property for CCTF-P, N-CQD/CCTF-P, S-CQD/CCTF-P, and N-SCQD/CCTF-P was evaluated using Zeta potential measurements. As shown in Fig. 3d, the Zeta potential of CCTF-P is 7.48 mV, while that of the N-CQD/CCTF-P, S-CQD/CCTF-P, and N-SCQD/CCTF-P is −8.45, −19.0, and −28.05 mV, respectively. The N-SCQD/CCTF-P has a more negative potential than the CCTF-P, N-CQD/CCTF-P, and S-CQD/CCTF-P, due to the large quantities of functional groups consisting of oxygen elements such as OH and COOH groups on the surface, as indicated by XPS and FT-IR analysis. Therefore, N-SCQD/CCTF-P has a powerful built-in electric field, which has appreciably improved charge separation and electron transportation.

FT-IR spectra for the chemical structure of the CCTF-P displays a broad peak at 3300–3500 cm−1 that corresponds to the amine groups of triazine derivatives (HTD) ( Fig. 4a). The specific peak at 1790 cm−1 in the PTCDA can be attributed to the anhydride carbonyl, which dramatically disappeared [47], and two imide carbonyl groups appearing at 1686 and 1584 cm−1 in the CCTF-P [59]. The vibrations observed at 1357 and 1272 cm−1 were linked with the stretching of the C˗N imide ring bonds. [47]. For the HTD sample, the bond at 803 cm−1 was connected to the triazine units [60]. The distinct band at 1400 cm−1 in the N-CQDs due to incorporation of numerous favorable amino groups within the N-CQDs (Fig. S3). In Addition, the peaks are positioned at around 1309,1403, 1574, 1682, and 2849 cm−1 associated with the C-O, CN, C-C, and CH3, respectively [61]. Furthermore, the extensive spectral range observed between 3100 and 3500 cm−1 can be characteristic the presence of OH, CH, and NH bond within each of the synthesized carbon dots [62]. As for S-CQD (Fig. S3), the peaks at 1134 and 1500 cm−1 were owing to CS and CC stretching vibrations, respectively [54]. In the case of N-SCQD, the identified bond at 2059 cm−1 is indicative of NCS group. The peaks located at 1700 and around 1403 cm−1 are attributed to CO and C-N stretch, respectively. The peak situated at 1011 cm−1 is suggestive of a CS vibration (Fig. S3) [63]. Once the decoration process was complete, the expected distinctive bonds of both CCTF and nanocarbon dots could be successfully visible (Fig. 4b). The peak at 811 cm−1 is thought to be caused by the vibration of the tri-s-triazine rings. The peak at 1100 cm−1 is believed to be due to the presence of CS in S-CQDs/CCTF-P, while the observed bond at 2059 cm−1 may be proved that the N-SCQD is well combined with the CCTF-P.

The interfacial charge transfer in the CCTF-P, N-SCQDs, and N-SCQDs/CCTF-P heterojunctions could be further evidenced by the binding energy shifts of XPS ( Fig. 5). The survey XPS spectrum of N-SCQDs/CCTF-P illustrates characteristic peaks of N-SCQDs/CCTF-P, including 287.8, 402.9, and 535.7 eV that are ascribed to C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s, respectively. The atomic weights% of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen were 85.5 %, 4.1 %, and 10.3 %, respectively. The C 1 s region of CCTF-P is commonly deconvoluted in two main contributions, i.e. both peaks of sp2 bond linked to CC (287.2 eV) and CN (289.0 eV). The N1s spectrum of CCTF-P could be fitted into two peaks (401.9 eV for triazine CN and 403.8 eV for imide nitrogen). In the case of the N-SCQDs, the six bonds displayed more importance in the C1s spectrum, due to the presence of CC, CN, C–N, CS, C–S, and OC–O− groups. Regarding the N1s region, three contributions can be ascribed, i.e. pyridinic (C–N–C), graphitic (N–C3), and pyrrolic (N–H). As for the S 2p spectrum, as shown in Fig. 5, the two types of chemical states with binding energies are located at 163.9 eV and 167.1 eV, which are responsible for the C-S and N = CS, respectively.

According to the C 1 s high-resolution XPS analysis of N-SCQDs/CCTF-P, the sp2 hybridized carbon types linked to C-S (278.0 eV), CC (287.1 eV), and CN (289.2 eV). The N 1 s spectrum indicates the presence of N-CO (403.8 eV) and CN (401.9 eV), which may be correspond to the imide nitrogen and triazine units, respectively [47]. Both main peaks of the O 1 s peak in N-SCQDs/CCTF-P appeared at 534.6 and 536.8 eV, that were introduced to C-O bond and CO bond, respectively [36]. A minor portion of sulfur was also found, binding energies of 163.5 eV (C-S), 167.1 eV (N = CS), and 172.0 eV (S-O) [64,65]. The N-SCQDs/CCTF-P heterojunction indicated that both the CCTF-P and N-SCQDs contributed to the aforementioned regions, resulting in a greater influence from the CCTF-P due to the higher proportion in comparison to the N-SCQDs in agreement with the EDX and FTIR results.

We also tested the CCTF-P sample utilizing ICP-OES and found that it's completely free of any residual zinc metal (Tables S2 and S3). This result demonstrates that the prepared polymer is in fact a non-metallic polymer.

The optical performances of photocatalysts are shown in Fig. 6. Fig. 6a and b demonstrate the UV-Vis and diffuse reflection spectrum (UV-DRS) of CCTF-P, N-CQD/CCTF-P, S-CQD/CCTF-P, and N-SCQD/CCTF-P photocatalysts. Remarkably, CCTF-P displays two wide absorption edges at ∼ 430 and 707 nm, corresponding to the bandgaps of about 2.01 and 1.31 eV from the Tauc plots (Fig. 6b). As anticipated, an extended conjugated π system of PTCDA at the fundamental unit would markedly enhance the light absorption potential of CCTF-P, enabling a considerable range of visible wavelengths to be absorbed. As you can see in Fig. 6a (inset), maximum absorption peaks for N-CQD, S-CQD, and N-SCQD are about 328, 398, and 372 nm, respectively. So, the N-CQD, S-CQD, and N-SCQD displayed the band gaps of around 2.83, 2.38, and 2.47 eV, respectively (Fig. S4). After CQDs were introduced, the CQDs/CCTF-P composites showed both shifts in absorbance intensity and also in visible light absorptions. This event indicates that CQDs/CCTF-P heterostructures exhibited narrow energy gap values, potentially leading to improved photoactivity by producing electron-hole pairs. Additionally, the band gap values indicate the presence of CQDs decreases the band gap from 2.01 for CCTF-P to 1.82, 1.62, and 1.60 eV for N-CQD/CCTF-P, S-CQD/CCTF-P, and N-SCQD/CCTF-P, respectively.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of CCTF-P, N-CQD/CCTF-P, S-CQD/CCTF-P, and N-SCQD/CCTF-P are shown in Fig. 7. The PL of CCTF-P shows a maximum wavelength at 597 nm, which is the band-to-band recombination of the electrons and holes. N-CQD/CCTF-P and S-CQD/CCTF-P had weaker peaks for better inhibition of the electrons and hole recombination. The lowest emission intensity was introduced via N-SCQD/CCTF-P photocatalyst, proving that the unique characteristics of N-SCQD facilitated the significant migration of excited-state electrons and this subject encourage their engagement in the OHC photocatalytic process. In addition, a progressive shift can be observed from 597 nm to 606 nm, highlighting the improved stabilization for the photo-generated electrons (e-) and heles (h+) due to the switch of fluorescent originating from various functional groups or a wide distribution of N-SCQDs, which is too slight for the naked eye to observe [66,67].

The N-SCQD/CCTF-P photocatalyst depicted the most minimal interfacial charge resistance (Rct∼0.86 kΩ) among all the photocatalysts, as evidenced by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) ( Fig. 8a). This event indicates a faster electron separation process that can be comfortably transferred out through N-SCQD/CCTF-P.

As illustrated in Fig. 8b, the photocurrent response of N-SCQD/CCTF-P under visible light irradiation is about 1.5 times higher than that of CCTF-P, N-CQD/CCTF-P, and S-CQD/CCTF-P, indicating that the photogenerated carriers’ separation has been enormously accelerated owing to the creation of a non-homogeneous structure between N-SCQD and CCTF-P. Also, the photocurrents of CCTF-P, N-CQD/CCTF-P, S-CQD/CCTF-P, and N-SCQD/CCTF-P first develop quickly and then reduce gradually under chopped illumination, which could be assigned to the charge and discharge of the electric double layer during the electrode polarization process. Additionally, the electrochemical results indicate that the introduction of N-CQD, S-CQD, and N-SCQD into the CCTF-P system can accelerate the electron transfer and thus inhibit the charge recombination efficiently, and consequently, certain synergistic effect existed between these components [68–70].

The Mott-Schottky analysis indicates that all of the N-CQD, S-CQD, N-SCQD, and CCTF-P samples exhibit positive slopes, indicative of n-type semiconductor characteristics. Furthermore, the Ef potentials derived from the analysis of N-CQD, S-CQD, N-SCQD, and CCTF-P were found to be 0.83, 0.68, 0.79 and −0.90 V (vs Ag/AgCl) (Fig. 8c), which is approximately equivalent to 0.63, 0.48, 0.59 and −0.703 V (vs NHE). The values obtained for the conduction band potentials (EVB) referred to as NHE were 3.46, 2.87, 3.08, 1.307 V for N-CQD, S-CQD, N-SCQD, and CCTF-P, respectively, by the addition of the energy of the bandgap obtained by DRS-UV–vis and the Tauc plot method (VVB = VCB + EBG). Alternatively, the valence band edges for N-SCQD and CCTF-P have been estimated by the XPS analysis, recording at low binding energy values (Fig. 8d). As Fig. 8e clearly shows, the arrangement of the bands of the N-SCQD, and CCTF-P were found to be cross-shaped, which is consistent with the characteristic charge transfer pattern observed in a Z-scheme heterojunction.

The petrochemical raw wastewater was extracted by utilizing hexane, and then GC-MS analysis procedure was conducted to detect some organic matter available in the raw wastewater. The outcomes of GC-MS analysis summarized in Table S4 displayed that there were up to 16 types of organic hydrocarbons (OHC), most of which included alkanes, poly-aromatic hydrocarbons, and other heterocyclic organic compounds, among which the main compounds included naphthalene, phenanthrene, phthalic acid, etc.

The opto-electro photocatalytic activity of the fabricated CCTF-P, N-CQD/CCTF-P, S-CQD/CCTF-P, and N-SCQD/CCTF-P photocatalysts was carried out with their ability to degrade organic hydrocarbons from petrochemical wastewater after 30 min of adsorption in dark conditions, and exposure to visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm), and the organic hydrocarbon concentration was analyzed using COD and gas chromatography. Utilizing CCTF-P, N-CQD/CCTF-P, S-CQD/CCTF-P, and N-SCQD/CCTF-P in darkness test resulted in the adsorption of 1.28 %, 0.6 %, 0.4 %, and 0.2 % (Fig. S5), displaying a low specific surface area of photocatalysts (Table S1). Under visible light irradiation, the N-SCQD/CCTF-P heterojunction degraded more than 92 % OHC during 90 min ( Fig. 9a and S6), which can compete with photocatalysts with high efficiency. Meantime, the degradation of OHC from petrochemical wastewater under visible light followed the order N-SCQD/CCTF-P ˃ S-CQD/CCTF-P ˃ N-CQD/CCTF-P ˃ CCTF-P after 90 min, which validates the findings of the optical specifications.

In addition, the pseudo-first order kinetic model was followed in the photodegradation experiments. The predicted values of the photocatalytic reaction rate constant (kOBS) by this model for CCTF-P, N-CQD/CCTF-P, S-CQD/CCTF-P, and N-SCQD/CCTF-P are 0.589 h−1, 0.769 h−1, 1.02 h−1, and 1.69 h−1, respectively. The decoration of the CCTF-P with N-SCQD owing to remarkable progress in the kinetics of OHC degradation. The kOBS of N-SCQD/CCTF-P was about 2.8 times higher than that of CCTF-P (Fig. 9b), indicating that a high concentration of reactive oxygen species was produced. The combination of CCTF-P with the N-CQD and S-CQD also developed the degradation efficiency, however to a lesser extent than N-SCQD/CCTF-P.

The results obtained from other researchers have shown that waves that are produced via sonication can swiftly generate reactive oxidative types to degrade contaminants [71,72]. So, degradation of OHC was tested without visible light irradiation under the influence of ultrasound (40 Hz/240 W) using 0.03 g L−1 of N-SCQD/CCTF-P, and this result is shown in Fig. 9c. From these results, interestingly, there is a clear difference in the OHC degradation with visible light and sonication. Interestingly, OHC analysis revealed that just about 46 % of OHC was degradant within 90 min under sonication without the existence of visible light irradiation and did not display considerable sono-degradation, illustrating that reactive oxygen species production in this system is unpleasant.

To explore the degradation potential of CCTF-P, N-CQD/CCTF-P, S-CQD/CCTF-P, and N-SCQD/CCTF-P under sunlight condition, the N-SCQD/CCTF-P shows superior degradation performance for OHC, and the corresponding k value reaches to 2.34 h−1 (Fig. S7). Consequently, the removal efficiency of OHC under sunlight is enhanced, which demonstrates considerable economic value and potential for application in practical industrial wastewater treatment processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Key limiting factors to the Photocatalytic process
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of pH on photocatalytic activity of OHC investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 9d, the OHC degradation efficiency increased from 64.0 % to 92.2 % (kOBS value escalated from 0.673 h−1 to 1.723 h−1) within 90 min when the initial pH increased from 3.0 to 7.0, which might be attributed to the inhibitory influence of H+ and OH ions on the produced free radicals. Also, the pH values of the OHC solution could influence the surface potential of the N-SCQD/CCTF-P. The zeta potential of the N-SCQD/CCTF-P is about −28.05 when the pH is 0.7, which shows that electrostatic adsorption influences the adsorption of OHC on the N-SCQD/CCTF-P.

The effect of the photocatalyst dose was studied at different photocatalyst amounts from 0.01 g L−1 to 0.1 g L−1 at optimum pH for 90 min. The results revealed that the photodegradation of OHC improved from 54.5 % to 92.2 % when the dose of N-SCQD/CCTF-P was increased from 0.01 to 0.03 g L−1. Fig. 9e shows a significant effect of the photocatalyst dose variation on kOBS. At a photocatalyst dose of 0.03 g L−1, the top value of kOBS ∼ 1.72 h−1 was obtained. At lower or higher dosages than 0.03 g L−1, a drop in kOBS appears. This function can be attributed to the rise in the active positions of the N-SCQD/CCTF-P, which leads to the generation of large amounts of reactive oxygen species. But at higher doses of the N-SCQD/CCTF-P (0.05 g L−1 to 0.1 g L−1), the turbidity of the suspension was enhanced, and the penetration of visible light into the suspension diminished, which effected a decrement in OHC photodegradation performance.

Additionally, the impact of different temperatures on the breakdown of OHC via N-SCQD/CCTF-P was investigated at four standard temperatures in the range of 15–45 °C. The OHC photochemical process rose as the temperature improved, reaching 98.2 % at 45 °C compared to 85 % at 15 °C, with a corresponding increase in kOBS as depicted in Fig. 9f.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism disclosure of photocatalytic reaction
ce:section.ce:para:
Researchers used scavenger experiments to determine the types of reactive oxidative species (ROS) that play a significant role in the oxidation of OHC in the case of the N-SCQD/CCTF-P. Fig. 10a displays the various scavenger influences similar BQ, NaN3, TBA, AgNO3, and EDTA-2Na to capture superoxide radials (
O
2
• −
), singlet oxygen (1O2), hydroxyl radicals
( OH
•
)
, excited electrons (e-), and holes (
h
+
) on the survey of OHC during photocatalytic degradation with N-SCQD/CCTF-P under visible light irradiation. For the prevention of the probable
O
2
• −
contribution, BQ is mainly utilized because of its high rate of activity, i.e, (0.9–1) 109 M−1S−1 [73,74], and the result shows that OHC removal efficiency declined from 92.2 % to 32.5 %. The kOBS in the presence of BQ decreased 5.8 times compared to the main sample, which supported proof of the significant role of
O
2
• −
in OHC photocatalytic degradation. The influence of the 1O2 was analyzed by adding NaN3, which led to a 64 % decrease in the kOBS compared to the blank test. As shown in Fig. 10a, with EDTA-2Na addition, the removal performance of OHC compounds decreases by approximately 22.5 %, and the corresponding kOBS value reduces from 1.53 h−1 to 0.768 h−1, indicating that the photodegradation of OHC still proceeded without any significant reduction, expressing less
h
+
production or less contribution
h
+
to OHC degradation over N-SCQD/CCTF-P. Subsequently, an experiment was conducted to assess the potential of AgNO₃ to act as a quencher agent for photoproduced electrons. In the presence of AgNO3, the OHC photocatalytic degradation kinetic reduced the kOBS to 52 %. So, the role of excited electrons could be considered as important. On the other hand, TBA can impede the photodegradation of OHC, as indicated by a kOBS value of 0.405 h−1, thereby revealing the existence of
OH
•
as well as its contribution to the degradation of OHC. According to the above-obtained results, two acceptable photocatalytic mechanisms were offered for OHC photodegradation and schematically investigated in Fig. 10b. The valence and conduction band potentials were previously determined based on the flat band potential computed obtained through electrochemical Mott-Schottky evaluation, as well as the bandgap energy assessment (Fig. 8c and d).

As displayed in Fig. 10b, the CCTF-P structure with N-SCQD may be designed in accordance with a type-II band alignment heterojunction. The proposed scheme involves the exposure of CCTF-P to light, which would result in the transfer of excited electrons (e-) in its CB to N-SCQD. Concurrently, the transfer of photogenerated h+ in N-SCQD's VB to CCTF-P would facilitate the release of the charge that is created by light. In addition, the encouraged electrons (e-) located in the N-SCQD's conduction band (CB) failed to catalyze the oxidation of oxygen to
O
2
• −
. This was a consequence of the N-SCQD's conductive band (ECB), which is 0.59 eV, exhibiting a larger value compared to the O2/
O
2
• −
reduction value, which is −0.33 V [75]. The existing h+ on the CCTF-P's valence band (VB) was inadequate to undergo oxidation of H2O to
OH
•
due to the higher negative value of the CCTF-P (1.307 eV) with respect to the H2O/
OH
•
potential (1.99 V) [76], which was inconsistent with the results of the scavenger tests. Accordingly, the direct Z-scheme mechanism appears to be a more appropriate option regarding N-SCQD/CCTF-P photocatalyst in comparison with the II-scheme mechanism.

The e- are organized along the N-SCQD's conduction band (CB), where they combine with the h+ present in the CCTF-P's valence band (VB). The generated e- in the N-SCQD's conduction band (CB) can migrate with ease to the CCTF-P's valence band (VB), where they form a recombination electrostatically with the h+ that have been produced [77]. It can be demonstrated that the excited e- within the CCTF-P's conduction band (-0.703 eV) is capable of reducing O2/
O
2
• −
due to the ECB exhibiting a lower potential compared to the O2 and
O
2
• −
standard reduction (-0.33 V) [75]. As well, the photogenerated h+ in the N-SCQD's valence band (VB) (3.08 eV) can act as a catalyst for the conversion of H2O into
OH
•
, due to the N-SCQD's valence band potential (EVB) being at a higher potential than the standard reduction potential of the H2O/
OH
•
(1.99 V) [76]. Hence, these reactive oxygen species (
O
2
• −
, 1O2, e-,
and
OH
•
) can significantly degrade and mineralize OHC. Hence, the OHC photocatalytic degradation system by N-SCQD/CCTF-P could be ascribed to the configuration of the Z-scheme heterostructure design. Also, in the case of N-CQD/CCTF-P and S-CQD/CCTF-P, the double Z-scheme mechanism is a better option for heterojunction structures due to the increased redox ability of electrons and holes as similarly delineated in the N-SCQD/CCTF-P example (Fig. S8).

In this section, degradation paths of petrochemical wastewater using a photocatalytic degradation process are speculated using GC-MS combined with literature [78,79]. There are many types of organic matter in petrochemical raw wastewater, and some organic compounds cannot be extracted by hexane or identified by GC-MS analysis. Table S5 represents the molecular structure, molecular formula, and residence time of the principal organic compounds in petrochemical wastewater after the photocatalytic degradation process. As shown in Table S5 and Fig. 11, the outcomes of GC-MS analysis under optimal reaction conditions explain that there were purely four types of organic matter in the sample, which interprets that the number of organic compounds after the photocatalytic process was remarkably decreased. The molecular structure of organic compounds was comparatively simpler. This proposes that during the treatment of the OHC via photocatalytic process, the 2-ring OHC like naphthalene and the 3-ring and 4-ring OHC like phenanthrene were presumably to be attacked by free radicals (
O
2
• −
,
OH
•
, and
h
+
) followed by ring opening, and eventually change to non-toxic small-molecule organic matter.

So, several important organic compounds that were present in raw wastewater and were removed after the photocatalytic degradation process with N-SCQD/CCTF-P such as naphthalene, phenanthrene, and diethyl phthalate, were selected to speculate on their feasible degradation paths, as shown in Scheme 1. For naphthalene, the direct oxidation of naphthalene by hydroxylation has happened and changed it to intermediate 1 (m/z = 148), then ring break after being attacked by
OH
•
and h+ and due to the demethylation reaction, converting it into intermediate 2 (m/z = 173). Intermediate 3 (m/z = 167) was obtained via high oxidizability of
OH
•
,
O
2
• −
, and h+ and in the sequence, intermediates 4 (m/z = 150), 5 (m/z = 122), 6 (m/z = 106), and 7 (m/z = 78) are produced owing to a dehydroxy reaction. After that, due to the attack of
OH
•
radicals, intermediate 8 (m/z = 96) generates and an open ring reaction occurs to create intermediate compound 9 (m/z = 114) and 10 (m/z = 82) which at the end mineralize into CO2 and H2O [11,80].

Based on the photodegradation intermediates acquired in Scheme 1, phenanthrene’s aromatic rings are attacked by
OH
•
radicals to generate intermediate 1 (m/z = 214). Intermediates 2 (m/z = 214) and 3 (m/z = 214) [81] were formed by
O
2
• −
, and then both intermediates reacted with
OH
•
and
O
2
• −
radicals and led to the formation of monoethyl phthalate (m/z = 194) [82]. Eventually, these intermediates were subsequently oxidized to light mass and converted to CO2 and H2O.

The aliphatic chain and aromatic ring of diethyl phthalate could be easily affected via
OH
•
(Scheme 1), and consequently leads to the formation of phthalic acid as intermediate 1 with m/z = 166 and also intermediate 2 (ethyl salicylate, m/z = 166) [83]. Intermediate 3 (salicylaldehyde, m/z = 122) is generated from intermediate 2 owing to the impact of
OH
•
available in the solution, and finally phthalic acid and salicylaldehyde, by ring opening and hydroxylation process, decay into relatively simple ester and, at the end, to CO2 and H2O [84].

Furthermore, the TOC analysis demonstrated that the OHC breakdown reached 50.2 %, 57.3 %, 66.3 %, and 79 % for CCTF-P, N-CQD/CCTF-P, S-CQD/CCTF-P, and N-SCQD/CCTF-P, respectively, within 90 min. The results of this study validate the high degree of mineralization observed in OHC in response to visible light stimulation (Fig. S9).

Examinations were also conducted to evaluate the potential risk of OHC in aquatic ecosystems by examining the toxicity of OHC and its degradation byproducts from the N-SCQD/CCTF-P process. The findings are displayed in Table 2. We evaluated the short-term and chronic harmful effects of OHC and its by-products in line with Chinese and EU standards which cover lately created chemicals. These standards show that acute and chronic toxicities are both expressed in mg L−1 [64]. The level of toxicity of byproducts were significantly less toxic to fish, daphnia, and green algae. It is essential to highlight that the characteristics of these wastewater components and the manner of their transformation influence the efficacy of N-SCQD/CCTF-P in actual wastewater containing OHC when it is in conjunction with other water components. As a result, pilot-scale N-SCQD/CCTF-P studies need to be performed to explore the toxicity assessment of OHC in real sewage to provide more comprehensive knowledge.

The evaluation of long-term toxicities is according to the Chinese hazard evaluation criteria for novel chemical compounds (HJ/T154–2004), that categorizes them as follows: ChV < 0.1 (highly toxic), 0.1 < ChV < 1 (toxic), 1 < ChV < 10 (harmful) and ChV > 10 (non-harmful). In contrast, the European Union's criteria in Annex VI of Directive 67/548/EEC determine short-term toxicity, that is classified as: LC50 < 1 or EC50 < 1 (very toxic), 1 < LC50 < 10 or 1 < EC50 < 10 (toxic), 10 < LC50 < 100 or 10 < EC50 < 100 (harmful), LC50 > 100 or EC50 > 100 (not harmful).

The N-SCQD/CCTF-P reusability is a critical parameter in its utility in the degradation of pollutants from water [85,86]. Thus, in the OHC photocatalytic degradation, the stabilities of N-SCQD/CCTF-P were investigated during 5 cycles ( Fig. 12). As illustrated in Fig. 12a, the performance of OHC photodegradation decreased marginally from 92.2 % to 86.0 % after 5 cycles, describing the amazing reusability of this photocatalytic system. The degree of degradation remained unaltered, and most notably, the kinetics demonstrated minimal decline. The coefficient rates of pseudo-first order model for the reduction in OHC were found to be 1.66 ± 0.08 h−1 (run 1), 1.71 ± 0.08 h−1 (run 2), 1.65 ± 0.08 h−1 (run 3), 1.5 ± 0.08 h−1 (run 4), and 1.39 ± 0.08 h−1 (run 5). The structural properties of N-SCQD/CCTF-P were analyzed before and after the photocatalytic reaction (Fig. 12c–d), and no apparent change was observed. Fig. 12c demonstrates the XRD pattern of the fresh and used N-SCQD/CCTF-P. As can be observed, no clear shifts of the diffraction peaks were observed, with preservation of the nanosheet-like structure, indicating the stable crystalline structure of N-SCQD/CCTF-P. Moreover, the XPS full spectra of the recovered N-SCQD/CCTF-P heterojunction had no remarkable alteration compared with the fresh sample (Fig. 12d).

Table 3 compares the photocatalytic performance of N-SCQD/CCTF-P with various other photocatalysts that have been previously reported in order to assess the originality of this work in OHC photodegradation. Although, relatively high OHC degradation rate than 92.2 % can be obtained, which is a significant efficiency, but the noteworthy point is that in the present study, real petrochemical wastewater was used, which contains a combination of different hydrocarbons and is simultaneously removed by the N-SCQD/CCTF-P in the process of photocatalytic degradation in a short time. In addition, the low BOD/COD level of the used wastewater which is about 0.34 indicates that the real petrochemical wastewater has compounds with a hard structure that is not biodegradable and must have been treated with advanced chemical and oxidation methods. While in other researches, usually only one hydrocarbon is removed in water or wastewater samples, which can be concluded that the synthesized photocatalyst has excellent photocatalytic properties and also additionally, it has been shown to exhibit outstanding photocatalytic activity under visible light and has the potential for practical application in the complete degradation of organic hydrocarbons. This is due to its simple preparation and easy recovery process, making it suitable for industrial use.

In summary, conjugated covalent triazine-based framework nanosheets have been developed as metal-free heterogeneous photocatalyst for surface-anchoring CQDs to prepare CQDs/CCTF-P heterojunctions, which are superior photocatalysts for OHC degradation from wastewater exposed to visible light irradiation. The decorating of the CQDs on the CCTF-P surface caused a reduction in the energy bandgap, a low charge recombination rate, and better transfer of the electronic photogenerated charges. However, the bandgap of the samples verified the order N-SCQD/CCTF-P > SCQD/CCTF-P P > N-CQD/CCTF-P > CCTF-P, the sample N-SCQD/CCTF-P showed less electrical resistance and high visible light harvesting, resulting in superior photocatalytic performance, which is 2.8 times faster than CCTF-P. Moreover, quenching tests confirmed that the combined impact of both charge and energy transfers due to the conjugated system allows the N-SCQD/CCTF-P nanosheets to produce
O
2
• −
and 1O2 species for improved photocatalytic oxygen activation. The N-SCQD/CCTF-P can be stably reused in cycles with continuous high photocatalytic activity without a decrease in performance. This work furnishes new ideas for solving water treatment and clean energy production problems in the future with novel Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts.

Habibollah Younesi: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology. Nader Bahramifar: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Mohammad Ali Zolfigol: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision. Raziyeh Zandipak: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation.
